**My Dearest Eleanor,**

**I write this with the ink of the storm, the sea howling at my door, the wind tearing at the very stones of this lighthouse. I do not know if I shall see dawn. But I write, for I must.**

**I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet the silence between us has been louder than any storm. I have heard the sea's whispers, the crows' cries, the howls of the night, and none have spoken to me as the silence of your absence. I have tried, Eleanor, to find the words that might bridge the chasm between us. But the words have failed me, as have the tides.**

**You were once my light, my guiding star, the reason I kept the lantern burning when the world was dark. Now, I fear I have let it dim. I fear I have let it dim.**

**I have seen you in dreams, Eleanor. Not as you were, but as you might have been—brighter, stronger, more whole. I have seen you in the eyes of the storm, in the fury of the waves, in the heart of the tempest. And in those visions, I have seen you not as the daughter who left, but as the daughter who might return.**

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I sit by the light, as I have done for many a year, and the sea howls and roars in its wrath. The storm that now approaches is no mere tempest—it is a specter of the soul, a test of the spirit, and I fear I may not see the dawn.

But I must write, for the ink is still upon my hand, and the quill, though worn, still sings of you.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and time has done much to our hearts. I have watched the stars from this tower, and in their light, I have found solace, yet my heart has not been whole.

I have kept the light burning, for you, though you did not return, and I have kept the flame alive, for the love we once shared, though the world has turned its back.

I have heard the waves, and in their rhythm, I have found peace, yet my soul has not been at rest.
